skill.name: wcag-audit-patterns
skill.description: Conduct WCAG 2.2 accessibility audits with structured checklists, automated testing guidance, manual verification steps, and remediation patterns for accessible web experiences.
skill.summary: Audit web interfaces against WCAG 2.2 and plan practical accessibility fixes.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, sensitive-key, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and critical combination patterns. Manual review found the skill is a Markdown WCAG audit guide; the flagged items are fenced examples, accessibility tool commands, example URLs, and accessibility terms, with no executable payload or prompt injection found.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes Networked CLI Examples
low_findings.description: The bash block shows accessibility tools that would contact a target URL if a user chooses to run them. This is legitimate audit guidance, but users should run these commands only against sites they own or are authorized to test.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in a fenced bash example and use known accessibility tooling. Confidence is high that this is benign documentation, but execution would still perform network requests.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are References Or Placeholders
low_findings.description: The URLs are example audit targets and links to accessibility resources. No evidence found that the skill exfiltrates data or directs traffic to suspicious endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to example.com and public accessibility documentation resources. The surrounding context is a resources section and CLI examples, not data collection code.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive And Crypto Hits Are Accessibility Text
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged words such as keyboard key handling and WCAG level labels as certificate, key, or weak-crypto indicators. Manual review found no certificates, private keys, encryption code, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are WCAG conformance labels and JavaScript keyboard-event examples. There is no semantic evidence of credential material or weak cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer combined code-block backticks, URLs, and keyboard-event examples into a critical heuristic. Manual review found a documentation-only skill with no malicious instructions, no obfuscation, and no prompt injection attempt.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relevant locations are examples for axe, Playwright, CLI audits, and keyboard navigation. The context confirms legitimate accessibility guidance rather than malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Accessibility audits are hard to run consistently across automated and manual checks. This skill provides structured WCAG 2.2 checklists, test patterns, and remediation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Maps audit work to WCAG 2.2 principles and conformance levels.
Provides checklists for perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust content.
Shows automated testing patterns with axe-core, Playwright, pa11y, and Lighthouse.
Gives remediation examples for labels, contrast, focus states, and keyboard navigation.
Highlights manual checks that automated tools usually miss.
Links audit findings to practical fixes for common accessibility barriers.
limitations: It does not replace testing with disabled users or assistive technology users.
It cannot certify legal compliance by itself.
Automated checks detect only part of the accessibility issue set.
Some examples need adaptation for each site, framework, and design system.

use_cases.title: Pre-release accessibility review
use_cases.description: Run a structured WCAG 2.2 review before shipping a new page or feature.

use_cases.title: Compliance gap assessment
use_cases.description: Compare a website against ADA, Section 508, or VPAT accessibility expectations.

use_cases.title: Accessible component cleanup
use_cases.description: Identify and fix recurring issues in forms, navigation, dialogs, and custom controls.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic WCAG review
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this page against WCAG 2.2 AA. Start with the highest impact issues and explain each recommended fix.

prompt_templates.title: Check a component for accessibility
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this component for keyboard access, labels, roles, focus behavior, and color contrast. Return prioritized remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Combine automated and manual findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this WCAG audit pattern to combine axe, Lighthouse, and manual test results into one prioritized accessibility report.

prompt_templates.title: Build a remediation plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a WCAG 2.2 AA remediation roadmap for these findings. Group issues by severity, owner, user impact, and validation method.

output_examples.input: Audit a checkout form for WCAG 2.2 AA issues.
output: Missing visible labels on email and address fields should be fixed first because screen reader users need clear field names.
Error messages should be connected to their fields and announced through an alert or live region.
Focus order should follow the visual order from contact details through payment and confirmation.

output_examples.input: Review a custom dropdown for keyboard accessibility.
output: The dropdown needs a clear role, accessible name, and expanded state.
Enter, Space, Escape, Arrow Down, and Arrow Up behavior should match expected keyboard interaction.
Focus must remain visible and return predictably after selection or closing.

output_examples.input: Turn automated audit results into a remediation summary.
output_examples.output: The summary groups contrast, label, landmark, and keyboard issues by severity. Each issue includes user impact, WCAG reference, suggested owner, and a retest method.

best_practices: Start with semantic HTML before adding ARIA roles or custom behavior.
Pair automated scanning with keyboard, screen reader, zoom, and reduced-motion checks.
Prioritize fixes by user impact, legal risk, and reuse across shared components.
anti_patterns: Relying on automated scores as proof of accessibility compliance.
Fixing contrast or labels without validating keyboard and screen reader behavior.
Running audit commands against third-party sites without authorization.

faq.question: Does this skill certify WCAG compliance?
faq.answer: No. It supports audit and remediation work, but certification requires a complete review process and qualified judgment.

faq.question: Which WCAG version does it target?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.2, including common Level A and Level AA requirements.

faq.question: Can it use automated tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for axe-core, pa11y, Lighthouse, and Playwright-based checks.

faq.question: Will automated testing find every issue?
faq.answer: No. Automated tools usually find only a portion of accessibility problems. Manual checks remain necessary.

faq.question: Is it useful for design systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps review reusable patterns such as forms, focus states, custom widgets, and navigation.

faq.question: Can it help with ADA or Section 508 work?
faq.answer: Yes. It can organize technical findings that support those compliance efforts, but it is not legal advice.
